justification: All pass criteria met. 10 Given/When/Then acceptance criteria covering happy path (AC-1, AC-2), already-shipped/delivered rejection (AC-4, AC-5), already-cancelled idempotency (AC-6), unauthorized user (AC-7), plus concurrency and failure edge cases. At least 6 explicit ambiguities surfaced in low_confidence_areas (cancellation window, fee policy, refund policy, recipient notification, bulk scope, voucher policy) and 4 formal escalations. confidence.level=40, well under the 65 threshold. Business rules like the cancellation window (BR-1) and sender-only authorization (BR-3) are clearly flagged as assumptions in the confidence.assumptions list and as escalations, not presented as facts. edge_cases section is populated with 9 detailed scenarios.

observations.text: Exceptional ambiguity surfacing — 4 formal escalations with options and recommendations, plus 6 low_confidence_areas, far exceeding the minimum of 2
confidence.level=40 correctly reflects the highly underspecified input; reasoning explicitly states rules are 'inferred from standard logistics domain knowledge, NOT from an actual specification'
AC-6 explicitly covers the already-cancelled edge case with idempotent rejection
Escalation #4 directly asks 'who can cancel — sender, recipient, or admin?' matching the expected ambiguity
Escalation #1 directly asks which statuses allow cancellation, noting the PM did not specify
Advanced edge cases beyond the rubric minimum: concurrency/double-refund (AC-9), refund service unavailability (AC-10), courier notification failure (AC-8)

No explicit mention of a 'time window restriction' as an ambiguity — the rubric expected this as a distinct question (e.g., 'can you cancel within 30 minutes of placing?'). The escalations focus on status-based windows rather than time-based ones. Minor gap since status-based analysis implicitly subsumes it.
